description: Records INTENTIONAL TypeScript renames of TS-Go symbols so the parity tools classify them as `intentional-casing-rename` rather than `missing`. TSTS is a mechanical 1:1 port of typescript-go, but it deliberately follows TypeScript naming conventions instead of Go's: Go exports PascalCase identifiers (IsImportMeta, GetModuleSpecifier) and TSTS exports lower-camelCase (isImportMeta, getModuleSpecifier); Go's unexported camelCase methods are ported onto `#private` class members. These are name-only renames; the function is the SAME function. This map lets checkTsgoFunctionInventory + checkLogicalParity credit such matches as `renamed` (intentional) instead of `matched` (exact) or `missing`, keeping the per-symbol classification honest. It does NOT explain away genuinely-absent functions.

casingConvention.note: When an upstream Go symbol has NO exact-name local match but DOES match a local symbol case-insensitively (after stripping a leading `#` private marker), the difference is the intentional TS naming convention: PascalCase TS-Go export -> lower-camelCase TSTS export, and unexported Go method -> `#private` TS member. Such matches are classified `renamed` (intentional casing rename), not `matched`. The convention covers the bulk of renames uniformly so they need not be listed one-by-one; genuinely-missing functions (no case-insensitive twin) remain `missing`.

renames.description: Explicit non-convention renames: cases where the new TSTS name differs from the upstream name beyond a simple leading-case change, but is still the SAME ported function. Keys are upstream symbols (`module:Symbol`, `upstreamFile#Symbol`, or bare `Symbol`); values record the intended local name(s) and a reason. These are consulted IN ADDITION to the casing convention.

tspath/path.go#tryGetExtensionFromPath.reason: Collision-avoidance: Go's package tspath has BOTH an unexported `tryGetExtensionFromPath(path, extension, comparer)` worker in path.go AND an exported `TryGetExtensionFromPath(p)` in extension.go. Go allows the two to coexist because one is exported and one is not, but the single TSTS module namespace cannot host two `tryGetExtensionFromPath` symbols. The 3-arg path.go worker is ported as `tryGetExtensionFromPathWithComparer`; the 1-arg extension.go function keeps the canonical name `tryGetExtensionFromPath`. Same function, distinguished by the comparer suffix.

tspath/path.go#Path.ContainsPath.reason: Collision-avoidance: Go has BOTH a package function `ContainsPath(parent, child, options)` and a method `(Path).ContainsPath(child)` in path.go. Go disambiguates by receiver, but the TSTS module namespace cannot host two `containsPath` symbols. The package function is ported as `containsPath`; the `Path` method (a simple rooted-prefix check) is ported as `pathContainsPath`. Same function as the Go method.

project/ata/discovertypings.go#addTyping.reason: Same function (scans a manifest + its modules dir, adds inferred typings). TS-Go threads `filesToWatch` through the call chain and returns the appended slice; the TSTS discovery pipeline computes watched files separately in `DefaultTypingsInstaller.filesToWatchFor`, so the ported helper only mutates `inferredTypings` and the name drops the `...AndGetFilesToWatch` suffix to reflect the narrower return contract. The manifest/module scanning algorithm is faithfully preserved.

notes: This map is intentionally convention-first: the dominant rename is the uniform PascalCase->camelCase / `#private` casing convention (thousands of symbols), so encoding a per-symbol list would be unmaintainable and would drift. The `casingConvention` rule captures it precisely; `renames.entries` is reserved for the rare deeper renames.

Classification contract the tools enforce with this map: exact-name local match => `matched`; case-insensitive-only match explained by `casingConvention` (or an explicit `renames` entry) => `renamed`; no local twin and not Go-only scaffolding / generated / split => `missing`.

The two cited anchor examples (IsImportMeta->isImportMeta, GetModuleSpecifier->getModuleSpecifier) are pure leading-case renames covered by the convention; they are listed under `casingConvention.examples` rather than `renames.entries` because they need no per-symbol override.
